lessons.subject: ENGLISH
lessons.slo:
By the end of the lesson, the learner should be able to:
a) Identify inanimate characters in a given poem.
b) Describe inanimate characters present in a given poem.
c) Appreciate the role of inanimate characters in poetry.
lessons.sle:
Learners are guided to:
• Identify inanimate characters in a poem.
• Find out the meaning of inanimate characters from print and digital texts.
• Discuss inanimate characters
likely in a poem.
• Read a poem related to the theme.
• Discuss the traits of inanimate.
• Role play an inanimate character
in a given poem.
• Relate inanimate characters with
individuals in real life.
lessons.kiq:
1. How do you describe the characters in a
poem?
2. Why are the characters in a poem important?
lessons.resources:
~ Course book- Skills in English Learners book and Teachers Guide.
~ Poetry books.
~ Electronic and digital devices.
~ Dictionaries.
lessons.orglearn: Learners are organised in pairs and later in groups.
lessons.intro: Introduce learners to the concept of inanimate characters in literature, explaining what they are and giving examples before linking to the theme.

stepDescs.Step 1: In pairs, learners identify inanimate characters in a given poem and list them, supporting each identification with a line from the poem.
stepDescs.Step 2: In pairs, learners find out the meaning of inanimate characters from print and digital texts, noting what role they play in literature.
stepDescs.Step 3: In groups, learners discuss inanimate characters likely to feature in a poem about domestic tourism, giving reasons for their choices.
stepDescs.Step 4: Individually, learners read the given poem related to the theme silently and identify all the inanimate characters used and what they represent.

lessons.extended: Learners find other poems featuring inanimate characters from print or digital sources, identify the characters and explain their significance in the poem.
lessons.conclusion: Learners take an assignment to answer comprehension questions based on the poem, focusing on the inanimate characters used.
lessons.skill:
i. Identifying inanimate characters in a poem.
ii. Finding the meaning of inanimate characters from print and digital texts.
iii. Reading a poem and identifying inanimate characters and their role.
lessons.workdone:
i. Identified inanimate characters in a given poem and supported with text evidence.
ii. Found the meaning of inanimate characters from print and digital sources.
iii. Read the poem and identified all inanimate characters and what they represent.
lessons.asm:
- Reading aloud.
- Question and answer.
- Learner summaries of what they read.
- Peer assessment.
- Self-assessment and standardized reading assessments.

lessons.slo:
By the end of the lesson, the learner should be able to:
a) Identify polite expressions used in the introduction of self and others.
b) Use polite expressions in the introduction of self and others in different speaking contexts.
c) Model respectful behaviour during introductions.
lessons.sle:
Learners are guided to:
• Make a list of necessary details about people that one needs to know for effective introduction.
• Brainstorm different types of introduction.
• List polite expressions that can be used during the introduction.
• Match polite expressions to corresponding types of introduction.
• Discuss reasons for using polite language in introductions.
• Use games such as 'catch the ball' for the introduction of others.
• Role-play different contexts of self-introduction with peers.
• Review an audio or video recording of formal and informal introductions.
lessons.kiq: 1. 1. Why is it important for people to introduce themselves?
lessons.resources:
~ Course book- Skills in English Learners book and Teachers Guide.
~ Electronic and digital device.
~ Video clip.
~ Audio-visual resource.
lessons.orglearn: Learners are organised in pairs and later in groups.
lessons.intro: Lead learners in a discussion on why polite language matters during introductions, linking the lesson to the theme of Personal Responsibility.

stepDescs.Step 1: Learners make a list of necessary details about people that one needs to know for an effective introduction and share their lists with a partner.
stepDescs.Step 2: In groups, learners brainstorm different types of introduction (self-introduction, introducing a friend, introducing a guest) and note examples of each.
stepDescs.Step 3: Learners list polite expressions that can be used during introductions and match each expression to the corresponding type of introduction.
stepDescs.Step 4: In groups, learners discuss reasons for using polite language in introductions, sharing personal experiences where polite introductions made a difference.

lessons.conclusion: Learners are guided to summarise the lesson by sharing one polite expression they will use the next time they introduce themselves.
lessons.extended: Learners search print and digital sources for more polite expressions used in introductions, list them and share five examples in the next lesson.
lessons.skill:
i. Listing details needed for an effective introduction.
ii. Brainstorming types of introduction and polite expressions used.
iii. Matching polite expressions to types of introduction.
lessons.workdone:
i. Listed necessary details needed for an effective introduction.
ii. Brainstormed types of introduction and listed polite expressions for each.
iii. Matched polite expressions to the corresponding type of introduction.
lessons.asm:
- Role play.
- Oral discussions.
- Dialogues.
- Peer assessment.
- Self-assessment and standardized listening assessments.

lessons.orglearn: Learners are organised in groups and later in pairs.
lessons.intro: Learners are guided to review the previous lesson on polite expressions and types of introduction and link it to this lesson on using the expressions in role plays.

stepDescs.Step 1: In pairs, learners use a game such as "catch the ball" for the introduction of others, using polite expressions they listed in the previous lesson.
stepDescs.Step 2: In pairs, learners role play different contexts of self-introduction, using polite expressions appropriate for each context.
stepDescs.Step 3: Learners review an audio recording of introductions and evaluate whether the polite expressions used were appropriate for each context.
stepDescs.Step 4: In pairs, learners take turns to introduce each other to the class using polite expressions, then give peer feedback on each other's introductions.

lessons.conclusion: Learners are guided to summarise the lesson by answering what they have learnt about using polite language in introductions.
lessons.extended: Learners practise introducing family members to each other at home, using polite expressions learnt in class, and report on the experience in the next lesson.
lessons.skill:

ii. Role playing different contexts of self-introduction.

lessons.slo:
By the end of the lesson, the learner should be able to:
a) Identify a variety of texts for independent reading.
b) Read selected materials for information and enjoyment.
c) Appreciate the value of independent reading in lifelong learning.
lessons.sle:
Learners are guided to:
• Think-pair-share on the factors to consider when selecting reading material.
• Select appropriate reading materials related to personal responsibility.
• Set and share reading goals for the session.
• Read selected print and non-print materials independently.
• Observe good reading habits.
• Keep a portfolio or a journal of their experiences during the reading session.
• Conduct peer review of the portfolios and journals kept.
lessons.kiq: 1. 1. How can you ensure that you benefit from a reading session?
lessons.resources:
~ Course book- Skills in English Learners book and Teachers Guide.
~ Electronic and digital devices.
~ Storybooks.
~ Journals.
lessons.orglearn: Learners are organised in pairs and later in groups.
lessons.intro: Explain to learners what extensive reading and independent reading mean and why reading widely is important, linking to the theme of Personal Responsibility.

stepDescs.Step 1: In pairs, learners think-pair-share on the factors to consider when selecting reading material, listing what they consider most important.
stepDescs.Step 2: Learners select appropriate reading materials related to personal responsibility from the options available in class (print and digital).
stepDescs.Step 3: In pairs, learners set and share reading goals for the session, deciding what they want to find out from their selected reading material.
stepDescs.Step 4: Individually, learners read selected print and non-print texts on personal responsibility, making brief notes on what they read.

lessons.conclusion: Learners take an assignment to continue reading their selected text at home and note three key points to share in the next lesson.
lessons.extended: Learners search their home or local library for reading materials on personal responsibility and begin reading one text of their choice.
lessons.skill:
i. Identifying factors to consider when selecting reading material.
ii. Selecting appropriate reading materials related to the theme.
iii. Reading selected texts and making notes on key points.
lessons.workdone:
i. Identified factors to consider when selecting reading material.
ii. Selected appropriate reading materials related to personal responsibility.
iii. Read selected texts and made brief notes on key points.
lessons.asm:
- Learner summaries of what they read.
- Learner journals.
- Peer assessment.
- Self-assessment and standardized reading assessments.
- Keeping a record of books read.

a) Identify a variety of texts for independent reading.
b) Read selected materials for information and enjoyment.
c) Appreciate the value of independent reading in lifelong learning.

~ Storybooks.

lessons.orglearn: Learners are organised in groups and later individually.
lessons.intro: Learners are guided to review the previous lesson on selecting and reading materials and link it to this lesson on sharing what they have read and creating a personal reading journal.

stepDescs.Step 1: In groups, learners share what they read in their selected texts, discussing the key points and what they found interesting.
stepDescs.Step 2: Individually, learners record their reading in a personal journal, noting the title, author, what it is about and what they learnt from it.
stepDescs.Step 3: In pairs, learners compare their reading journals, giving each other feedback on the reading entries made.
stepDescs.Step 4: Individually, learners write a short personal response to the text they read, stating what they enjoyed and what they would recommend to a friend.

lessons.conclusion: Learners are guided to summarise the lesson by answering what they have learnt about reading independently and keeping a reading journal.
lessons.extended: Learners continue updating their personal reading journal, recording all texts they read over the next two weeks.
lessons.skill:
i. Sharing what was read and discussing key points in groups.
ii. Recording reading in a personal journal.
iii. Writing a personal response to a text read.
lessons.workdone:
i. Shared key points from reading in groups.
ii. Recorded reading in a personal journal with title, author and key points.
iii. Wrote a personal response to the text read.
lessons.asm:
- Learner summaries of what they read.
- Learner journals.
- Peer assessment.

lessons.slo:
By the end of the lesson, the learner should be able to:
a) Identify the different types of nouns from a print or digital text.
b) Use different types of nouns in sentences.
c) Appreciate the role of correct grammar in written and spoken communication.
lessons.sle:
Learners are guided to:
• Read a print or non-print text.
• Identify common.
• And abstract nouns.
• Work with peers to search online for examples of the common.
• And abstract nouns.
• And compile a list of the nouns.
• Listen to a poem or a story and categorise the nouns used.
• Mention examples of common.
• Concrete and abstract nouns in the classroom and school.
• Construct sentences using common.
• And abstract nouns.
• Complete substitution tables with the different types of nouns.
• Engage in language games such as scrabble.
• Code words.
• And guessing games.
• Write and display stories.
• Or poems featuring the different types of nouns.
lessons.kiq: 1. 1. Why is it important to identify items by name?
lessons.resources:
~ Course book- Skills in English Learners book and Teachers Guide.
~ Electronic and digital devices.
~ Flash cards.
~ Charts.
lessons.orglearn: Learners are organised in pairs and later in groups.
lessons.intro: Link the lesson to learners' prior knowledge of nouns and explain that this lesson focuses on identifying common, proper, concrete and abstract nouns from texts.

stepDescs.Step 1: Individually, learners read a print or non-print text and identify common, proper, concrete and abstract nouns, underlining each one found.
stepDescs.Step 2: In pairs, learners search online for examples of common, proper, concrete and abstract nouns and compile a list of at least five examples for each type.
stepDescs.Step 3: In groups, learners sort the nouns they found into the four categories on a chart and display the chart for the class to view.
stepDescs.Step 4: Learners listen to the teacher read a short text and pick out the nouns heard, writing them down and categorising them.

lessons.conclusion: Learners take a written assignment on identifying the types of nouns in given sentences.
lessons.extended: In groups, learners search print and digital materials for texts rich in nouns, identify and categorise all nouns found and share their lists in the next lesson.
lessons.skill:
i. Identifying common, proper, concrete and abstract nouns from a text.
ii. Searching for and categorising examples of the four types of nouns.
iii. Listening to a text and picking out and categorising nouns.
lessons.workdone:
i. Read a text and identified common, proper, concrete and abstract nouns.
ii. Searched for and compiled examples of the four types of nouns in groups.
iii. Listened to a text and identified and categorised nouns heard.
lessons.asm:
- Gap-filling.
- Short-answer.
- Matching tasks.
- Peer assessment.
- Word games.
- Substitution tables.

lessons.resources:

lessons.orglearn: Learners are organised in groups and later individually.
lessons.intro: Learners are guided to review the previous lesson on identifying types of nouns and link it to this lesson on using different types of nouns in sentences.

stepDescs.Step 1: In groups, learners construct sentences using common, proper, concrete and abstract nouns in the context of personal responsibility, writing at least two sentences per type.
stepDescs.Step 2: In pairs, learners play a language game on nouns where one names a type of noun and the other provides an example and uses it in a sentence.
stepDescs.Step 3: Individually, learners fill in gaps in given sentences by inserting the correct type of noun from a word bank.
stepDescs.Step 4: In groups, learners create a word puzzle using the nouns they collected and exchange puzzles with another group to solve.

lessons.conclusion: Learners are guided to summarise the lesson by answering what they have learnt about the different types of nouns and how they are used.
lessons.extended: Learners write a short paragraph on personal responsibility using at least one of each of the four types of nouns, underlining each noun type used.
lessons.skill:
i. Constructing sentences using all four types of nouns.
ii. Playing a language game identifying and using nouns correctly.
iii. Filling in gaps using the correct type of noun.

ii. Played a language game on identifying and using types of nouns correctly.

lessons.slo:
By the end of the lesson, the learner should be able to:
a) Identify the main events in trickster narratives.
b) Analyse the characters in narratives.
c) Appreciate the importance of trickster narratives in the inculcation of values.
lessons.sle:
Learners are guided to:
• Engage in pre-reading activities such as previewing the title of the narrative.
• Using picture clues.
• And brainstorming.
• Recount the events in the trickster narrative and highlight the trick in the narrative.
• Discuss the various character traits displayed by the characters.
• Brainstorm on the moral lessons of the narrative.
• Work collaboratively to retell a trickster narrative.
• Relate characters and events in the trickster narrative to real-life situations.
• Collaborate with peers to explore and share how personal responsibility can be derived from the narrative’s moral lessons.
lessons.kiq: 1. 1. Why would we listen to trickster narratives?
lessons.resources:
~ Course book- Skills in English Learners book and Teachers Guide.
~ Storybooks.
~ Electronic and digital devices.
~ Dictionaries.
lessons.orglearn: Learners are organised in pairs and later in groups.
lessons.intro: Explain what trickster narratives are, giving examples from Kenyan oral literature, and introduce the trickster narrative to be read.

stepDescs.Step 1: Learners engage in pre-reading activities such as previewing the title, using picture clues and brainstorming what they expect the narrative to be about.
stepDescs.Step 2: Individually, learners read the trickster narrative silently, recount the events in the narrative and highlight the trick used in the story.
stepDescs.Step 3: In groups, learners discuss the various characters in the trickster narrative, focusing on what each character does and how each contributes to the trick.
stepDescs.Step 4: In pairs, learners discuss the lessons learnt from the trickster narrative and relate them to real-life situations involving personal responsibility.

lessons.conclusion: Learners take an assignment to answer comprehension questions on the trickster narrative they read in the lesson.
lessons.extended: Learners search print and digital sources for other trickster narratives from Kenyan oral literature, read them and note the characters and tricks used.
lessons.skill:
i. Engaging in pre-reading activities and predicting the narrative.
ii. Reading the trickster narrative and identifying the trick used.
iii. Discussing characters and lessons from the trickster narrative.
lessons.workdone:
i. Engaged in pre-reading activities and predicted the events of the narrative.
ii. Read the trickster narrative and identified the trick and sequence of events.
iii. Discussed characters and lessons learnt from the trickster narrative.
lessons.asm:
- Reading aloud.
- Question and answer.
- Learner summaries of what they read.
- Peer assessment.
- Self-assessment and standardized reading assessments.

lessons.slo:
By the end of the lesson, the learner should be able to:
a) Describe features of legible and neat handwriting for efficient writing.
b) Write texts legibly and neatly for effective communication.
c) Acknowledge the importance of writing neatly and legibly.
lessons.sle:
Learners are guided to:
• Listen to an audio clip on the importance of developing legible and neat handwriting.
• Study written samples of legible and neat handwriting featuring all letters of the alphabet and short texts.
• Discuss the features of legible and neat handwriting.
• Including shaping letters.
• Joining and spacing letters and words.
• Practise handwriting games such as blind writing.
• Speed writing.
• And letter stations.
• Suggest ways of correcting bad handwriting habits such as joining letters and words inappropriately.
• Copy given texts on the theme of personal responsibility on charts and display for peer evaluation and correction.
• Write short paragraphs legibly and neatly.
• And share with peers for peer review.
lessons.kiq: 1. 1. Why should we write legibly and clearly?
lessons.resources:
~ Course book- Skills in English Learners book and Teachers Guide.
~ Electronic and digital devices.
~ Manilla papers.
~ Journals.
lessons.orglearn: Learners are organised in pairs and later individually.
lessons.intro: Lead learners in a discussion on why legible and neat handwriting is important in communication and link it to the theme of Personal Responsibility.

stepDescs.Step 1: Learners listen to an audio clip on the importance of developing legible and neat handwriting, noting key points on what makes handwriting effective.
stepDescs.Step 2: Learners study written samples of legible and neat handwriting featuring all letters of the alphabet and short texts, and identify the features that make them effective.
stepDescs.Step 3: Individually, learners copy a short text provided by the teacher, paying attention to letter formation, spacing and alignment.
stepDescs.Step 4: In pairs, learners assess each other's handwriting for legibility and neatness, giving feedback on specific features that need improvement.

lessons.conclusion: Learners take an assignment to copy a passage from their course book at home, focusing on legibility and neatness.
lessons.extended: Learners practise writing neatly every day in their exercise books and keep a handwriting sample from each week to track improvement.
lessons.skill:
i. Identifying features of legible and neat handwriting from samples.
ii. Copying a text with attention to letter formation, spacing and alignment.
iii. Assessing a peer's handwriting for legibility and neatness.
lessons.workdone:
i. Identified features of legible and neat handwriting from written samples.
ii. Copied a short text paying attention to letter formation, spacing and alignment.
iii. Assessed a peer's handwriting for legibility and neatness.
lessons.asm:
- Learner portfolios.
- Peer assessment.
- Self-assessment.
- Portfolio.
- Standardized writing assessment.

lessons.slo:
By the end of the lesson, the learner should be able to:
a) Outline the oral narrative performance techniques for effective delivery.
b) Use oral narrative techniques during the performance.
c) Appreciate the importance of performance techniques in the successful delivery of oral material.
lessons.sle:
Learners are guided to:
• Watch live or recorded oral performances of narratives as a class.
• Search online or offline for information on performance techniques.
• And make presentations to peers.
• Discuss the oral performance techniques.
• Perform oral narratives while peers watch.
• And then give feedback.
• Compose oral narratives and suggest to peers the most suitable ways of performing them.
lessons.kiq: 1. 1. What makes one a good storyteller?
lessons.resources:
~ Course book- Skills in English Learners book and Teachers Guide.
~ Electronic and digital devices.
~ Audio-visual resources.
~ Journals.
lessons.orglearn: Learners are organised in groups and later in pairs.
lessons.intro: Introduce the lesson by discussing what oral narratives are and why performance techniques are important for effective delivery, linking to Science and Health Education.

stepDescs.Step 1: As a class, learners watch a live or recorded oral performance of a narrative and discuss what they observed about how the narrative was presented.
stepDescs.Step 2: In pairs, learners search online or offline for information on performance techniques and make short notes on at least three techniques they find.
stepDescs.Step 3: Learners discuss the oral performance techniques they found, sharing their notes and building a class list of the most effective techniques.
stepDescs.Step 4: In pairs, learners perform oral narratives related to science and health while peers watch, record and then give feedback on the performance techniques used.

lessons.conclusion: Learners are guided to summarise the lesson by answering what performance technique they found most useful and why.
lessons.extended: In groups, learners compose short oral narratives on science and health topics and suggest to peers the most suitable ways of performing them.
lessons.skill:
i. Watching an oral narrative performance and identifying performance techniques.
ii. Searching for and making notes on oral narrative performance techniques.
iii. Performing an oral narrative using effective performance techniques.
lessons.workdone:
i. Watched an oral narrative performance and observed the techniques used.
ii. Searched for and made notes on oral narrative performance techniques.
iii. Performed oral narratives and received peer feedback on performance techniques.
lessons.asm:
- Oral presentations.
- Peer assessment.
- Self-assessment and standardized listening assessments.
- Observation schedule.
- Oral discussions.

lessons.slo:
By the end of the lesson, the learner should be able to:
a) Distinguish between poems and other literary genres.
b) Recite a variety of simple poems for enjoyment.
c) Collaborate in poetry recitation for enjoyment and learning.
lessons.sle:
Learners are guided to:
• Listen and respond to live or recorded poetry recitation.
• Discuss what makes reading simple poems interesting.
• Pick out poems from a variety of texts and read them out aloud.
• Recite poems that address science and health issues such as HIV and AIDS.
• Provide feedback to peers and seek help where necessary.
lessons.kiq: 1. 1. How are poems different from other forms of literary genres?
lessons.resources:
~ Course book- Skills in English Learners book and Teachers Guide.
~ Poetry books.
~ Electronic and digital devices.
~ Dictionaries.
lessons.orglearn: Learners are organised in pairs and later in groups.
lessons.intro: Introduce simple poems to learners, explaining what makes them accessible and enjoyable, linking to the theme of Science and Health Education.

stepDescs.Step 1: Learners listen and respond to a live or recorded poetry recitation on science and health, noting what they enjoyed about the recitation.
stepDescs.Step 2: In groups, learners discuss what makes reading simple poems interesting, sharing their personal opinions and reactions to the poem they heard.
stepDescs.Step 3: Learners pick out poems from a variety of texts and read them aloud, practising expression, rhythm and appropriate speed.
stepDescs.Step 4: In pairs, learners recite poems that address science and health issues, taking turns and giving each other feedback on expression and clarity.

lessons.conclusion: Learners take an assignment to select one poem from their reading material and practise reading it aloud at home.
lessons.extended: Learners search print and digital sources for simple poems on science and health topics, read them and share a favourite with the class in the next lesson.
lessons.skill:
i. Listening and responding to a poetry recitation.
ii. Discussing what makes reading simple poems interesting.
iii. Reciting poems on science and health with appropriate expression.
lessons.workdone:
i. Listened and responded to a recorded poetry recitation on science and health.
ii. Discussed what makes reading simple poems interesting.
iii. Read and recited poems on science and health in pairs.
lessons.asm:
- Reading aloud.
- Oral presentations.
- Peer assessment.
- Self-assessment and standardized reading assessments.
- Learner journals.

lessons.slo:
By the end of the lesson, the learner should be able to:
a) Distinguish between poems and other literary genres.
b) Recite a variety of simple poems for enjoyment.
c) Collaborate in poetry recitation for enjoyment and learning.
lessons.sle:

• Discuss what makes reading simple poems interesting.

lessons.resources:

lessons.orglearn: Learners are organised in groups and later individually.
lessons.intro: Learners are guided to review the previous lesson on reading and reciting simple poems and link it to this lesson on identifying the message and theme of a poem.

stepDescs.Step 1: In groups, learners re-read a poem on science and health and identify the main message the poet is communicating.
stepDescs.Step 2: In groups, learners discuss the theme of the poem and how it relates to real-life situations in science and health education.
stepDescs.Step 3: In pairs, learners identify and discuss the stylistic devices used in the poem, such as repetition, rhyme and rhythm.
stepDescs.Step 4: Individually, learners write a short summary of the poem they read, covering the main message, theme and at least one stylistic device identified.

lessons.conclusion: Learners are guided to summarise the lesson by answering what they have learnt about reading and analysing simple poems.
lessons.extended: Learners write their own simple poem on a science or health topic using at least one stylistic device and share it with a classmate.
lessons.skill:

lessons.intro: Introduce the lesson by discussing why turn-taking and polite interruptions are important in conversations, linking to the theme of Drug and Substance Abuse.

stepDescs.Step 1: As a class, learners watch a live or recorded dialogue depicting turn-taking skills and interrupting politely, noting the verbal and non-verbal cues used.
stepDescs.Step 2: In pairs, learners practise interrupting someone politely using phrases such as "Excuse me..." or "I beg your pardon...", taking turns to practise.
stepDescs.Step 3: In groups, learners practise turn taking in a conversation on drug and substance abuse, using appropriate expressions for each turn.
stepDescs.Step 4: Learners watch short videos of conversations and identify instances of turn taking and polite interruptions, noting the expressions used.

lessons.conclusion: Learners are guided to summarise the lesson by sharing one polite interruption expression they found most useful.
lessons.extended: Learners watch a TV or radio discussion programme at home, note three instances of polite interruption and share in the next lesson.
lessons.skill:
i. Watching a dialogue and identifying verbal and non-verbal cues for turn taking.
ii. Practising polite interruptions using appropriate phrases.
iii. Identifying turn taking and polite interruptions in video conversations.
lessons.workdone:
i. Watched a dialogue and identified verbal and non-verbal cues for turn taking.
ii. Practised polite interruptions using appropriate phrases in pairs.
iii. Watched short videos and identified instances of turn taking and polite interruptions.
lessons.asm:
- Role play.
- Oral discussions.
- Dialogues.
- Peer assessment.
- Self-assessment and standardized listening assessments.

lessons.slo:
By the end of the lesson, the learner should be able to:
a) Identify verbal and non-verbal forms of polite interruptions.
b) Apply turn-taking skills in different speaking contexts.
c) Use verbal and non verbal cues to interrupt a conversation politely.
d) Acknowledge the importance of politeness in communication.
lessons.sle:
Learners are guided to:
• Watch a live or recorded dialogue depicting turn-taking skills and interrupting politely.
• Practise in pairs interrupting someone politely.
• Practise turn taking in a conversation with peers.
• Watch short videos of conversations or television programmes and identify instances of turn taking and polite interruptions.
• Listen to a radio talk show and pick out polite words and expressions.
• Write examples of polite words and expressions on sticky notes or flash cards and paste them on the wall or class noticeboard.
• Create posters showing polite expressions as well as ways of interrupting someone politely.
• Design posters on turn taking skills and polite interruptions and display them.
• Role-play a dialogue on the effects of drug and substance abuse.
• Recite a conversational verse (choral).
• Make an audio or video recording and share it on WhatsApp.
• Or social media.

lessons.intro: Introduce reading fluency by explaining its key aspects of accuracy, speed and expression, linking to reading texts on drug and substance abuse.

stepDescs.Step 2: Learners watch news clips from a favourite television programme and comment on the presenter's speed, accuracy and expression.
stepDescs.Step 3: In groups, learners brainstorm ways of improving reading fluency, creating a list of strategies to share with the class.
stepDescs.Step 4: In pairs, learners practise reading a passage on drug and substance abuse aloud, using appropriate speed, accuracy and expression.

lessons.conclusion: Learners are guided to summarise the lesson by sharing one strategy they will use to improve their reading fluency.
lessons.extended: Learners practise reading a text aloud at home three times, focusing on improving accuracy and expression with each reading.

lessons.intro: Learners are guided to review the previous lesson on identifying and categorising adverbs and link it to this lesson on using adverbs correctly in extended writing.

stepDescs.Step 1: In groups, learners read a passage on drug and substance abuse and fill in blanks with the correct adverb of time, place or manner from a word bank.
stepDescs.Step 2: In pairs, learners search online and offline for more examples of adverbs of time, place and manner in drug awareness texts.

lessons.extended: Learners write ten sentences on drug awareness, each containing an adverb of time, place or manner, and underline each adverb used.

lessons.orglearn: Learners are organised in groups and later in pairs.
lessons.intro: Introduce lullabies as a form of oral literature, explaining their role in Kenyan culture and how they convey messages, linking to the drug and substance abuse theme.

stepDescs.Step 1: Learners listen to audio recordings of lullabies from a digital device, noting the mood, tone and message of each lullaby listened to.
stepDescs.Step 2: Learners view videos of lullabies and identify instances of repetition, noting how repetition is used to reinforce the message.
stepDescs.Step 3: In groups, learners perform a lullaby they know or have listened to and make a recording of their group performance.
stepDescs.Step 4: In pairs, learners pick out the key messages communicated in the lullabies they listened to or performed, noting what each lullaby is teaching.

lessons.conclusion: Learners are guided to summarise the lesson by sharing one key message from a lullaby they listened to and how it relates to family life.
lessons.extended: Learners ask a family member to teach them a lullaby from their community, note its message and teach it to a classmate in the next lesson.

lessons.intro: Learners are guided to review the previous lesson on listening to and performing lullabies and link it to this lesson on sharing ideas on key messages in lullabies.

stepDescs.Step 1: In groups, learners share ideas on the key messages they identified from the lullabies, discussing what each lullaby is teaching about family and community values.

By the end of the lesson, the learner should be able to:
a) Recognise commonly misspelt words in texts.
b) Use commonly misspelt words correctly in written texts.
c) Value the relationship between accurate spelling and effective written communication.
lessons.sle:
Learners are guided to:
• Mention words that are difficult to spell correctly.
• Work with peers to search for commonly misspelt words from textbooks and online sources.
• Discuss ways of correcting commonly misspelt words.
• Correct texts with wrong spellings of irregular plural nouns (e.g. knife-knives; ox-oxen) and –ing verbs formed from verbs with final –e (e.g. love – loving).
• Explain to peers the effects of correct spelling on written communication.
• Engage in spelling games such as crossword puzzles.
• Write a story or dialogue on contemporary issues.
• For example.
• Keeping my neighbourhood safe.
• Using the commonly misspelt words and share it with peers for feedback.

lessons.intro: Lead a class discussion on why correct spelling is important in writing and introduce the focus on commonly misspelt words related to drug and substance abuse.

stepDescs.Step 1: In pairs, learners mention words that are difficult to spell correctly and share their lists with the class, noting the most commonly mentioned ones.

stepDescs.Step 4: Individually, learners correct texts with wrong spellings, rewriting each misspelt word correctly in their exercise books.

lessons.conclusion: Learners take a written assignment on spelling commonly misspelt words from the list they compiled in the lesson.
lessons.extended: Learners use a dictionary to find the correct spelling of ten words they commonly misspell and create a personal spelling guide.

lessons.intro: Introduce listening for details by explaining the difference between main ideas and supporting details, linking to the theme of Natural Resources: Forests.

stepDescs.Step 4: In pairs, learners listen and note the clues signalling details from a peer reading a passage on forests aloud.

lessons.conclusion: Learners are guided to summarise the lesson by sharing one signpost word they found most useful for identifying details.
lessons.extended: Learners listen to a documentary on forests at home, note the signpost words used and at least three details signalled by them.

lessons.slo:
By the end of the lesson, the learner should be able to:
a) Articulate correctly the consonant sounds/t/, /d/, /l/, /r/ in words articulate correctly vowel sounds /ʊ/ and /uː/ in words.
b) Pronounce words correctly using stress.
c) Distinguish the meaning of words based on stress.
d) Acknowledge the importance of correct pronunciation in oral communication.
lessons.sle:
Learners are guided to:
• Listen to audio clips and watch video clips with the sounds /t/.
• /ʊ/ and /uː/ in words.
• Articulate the following consonants and vowel sounds: /t/ as in toll.
• /d/as in doll.
• /l/ as in liver.
• /r/ as in river.
• /ʊ/ as in pull.
• /uː/ as in pool.
• Boot; watch an oral presentation and pick out words with the target sounds.
• Practise pronouncing words with the target consonant and vowel sounds in pairs.
• Sort words based on the pronunciation of the target.
• Consonant.
• And vowel sounds.
• Recite poems or sing songs on national heroes with the target sounds.
• Say tongue twisters with the target consonant and vowel sounds.
• Listen to audio featuring a variety of words and classify them in their word classes according to the stressed syllables.
• Engage in pronunciation drills on stress for distinguishing word class.
• Underline stressed syllables in words.
• Discuss the importance of correct pronunciation and use of stress in conveying meaning.
• Reflect on the target consonant and vowel sounds and identify those they struggle with for further practise.
lessons.kiq: 1. 1. Why should you pronounce words correctly? 2. Why do people find it difficult to pronounce some words? 3. In what ways can one improve own pronunciation?

lessons.sle:
Learners are guided to:
• Search from print and non-print sources for tongue twisters with some of the target sounds and say them for enjoyment.
• Watch a video or listen to a recording or conversation on outdoor games and group the words in a table according to the following target sounds: v/ as in very; /f/ as in ferry /n/ as in been an /ŋ/ as in being.
• Listen to.
• And model native speaker clips with the target sounds in words.
• Work with peers to practise the pronunciation of words with the target sounds and record themselves.
• Share the clips for peer review.
• Listen to an audio or a dramatised conversation on outdoor games in which Yes/No and WH questions are used and point out whether a rising or falling intonation is used.
• Practise articulation of Yes/No and WH- questions with the appropriate intonation in dialogues.
• Dramatise and record a dialogue using words with the target sounds as well as Yes/ No and WH- questions and share with peers for review.

THEME / STRAND / SUB-STRAND
Theme: –
Strand: Grammar in Use
Sub-strand: Personal pronouns.
SPECIFIC LEARNING OUTCOMES
By the end of the lesson, the learner should be able to: a) Identify personal pronouns in sentences. b) Use subject and object pronouns in sentences appropriately. c) Appreciate the value of proper use of pronouns.
KEY INQUIRY QUESTION(S)
1. 1. Why should we avoid unnecessary repetition?
LEARNING RESOURCES
~ Course book- Skills in English Learners book and Teachers Guide. ~ Electronic and digital devices. ~ Flash cards. ~ Charts.
ORGANISATION OF LEARNING
Class Organisation: Learners are organised in pairs and later in groups.
Introduction
Link the lesson to prior knowledge of pronouns and introduce personal and possessive pronouns, giving examples from natural resources and forest contexts.
Lesson Development
Step 1
In pairs, learners pick out personal and possessive pronouns from print and non-print texts on natural resources, underlining each one found.
Step 2
In groups, learners use personal pronouns to refer to people in a class discussion, practising subject and object personal pronouns.
Step 3
Individually, learners construct sentences using personal pronouns in the context of natural resources and forests.
Step 4
In pairs, learners take turns to read sentences containing personal pronouns and replace them with the correct possessive pronouns where applicable.
Extended Activities
Learners search print and digital texts on forests for examples of personal and possessive pronouns, list five examples of each and share in the next lesson.
Conclusion
Learners take a written assignment on filling in the correct personal or possessive pronoun in given sentences.
ASSESSMENT METHODS
- Gap-filling. - Short-answer. - Matching tasks. - Peer assessment. - Substitution tables. - Word games.
LESSON SKILL ASSESSED
i. Identifying personal and possessive pronouns from natural resources texts. ii. Using personal pronouns to refer to people in a class discussion. iii. Constructing sentences using personal pronouns on forests.
WORK DONE
i. Identified personal and possessive pronouns from natural resources texts. ii. Used personal pronouns to refer to people in a class discussion. iii. Constructed sentences using personal pronouns on natural resources.
REFLECTION

THEME / STRAND / SUB-STRAND
Theme: Tourism: Domestic Tourism
Strand: Reading: Literature
Sub-strand: Intensive Reading : Poetry
SPECIFIC LEARNING OUTCOMES
By the end of the lesson, the learner should be able to: a) Identify inanimate characters in a given poem. b) Describe inanimate characters present in a given poem. c) Appreciate the role of inanimate characters in poetry.
KEY INQUIRY QUESTION(S)
1. How do you describe the characters in a poem? 2. Why are the characters in a poem important?
LEARNING RESOURCES
~ Course book- Skills in English Learners book and Teachers Guide. ~ Poetry books. ~ Electronic and digital devices. ~ Dictionaries.
ORGANISATION OF LEARNING
Class Organisation: Learners are organised in pairs and later in groups.
Introduction
Introduce learners to the concept of inanimate characters in literature, explaining what they are and giving examples before linking to the theme.
Lesson Development
Step 1
In pairs, learners identify inanimate characters in a given poem and list them, supporting each identification with a line from the poem.
Step 2
In pairs, learners find out the meaning of inanimate characters from print and digital texts, noting what role they play in literature.
Step 3
In groups, learners discuss inanimate characters likely to feature in a poem about domestic tourism, giving reasons for their choices.
Step 4
Individually, learners read the given poem related to the theme silently and identify all the inanimate characters used and what they represent.
Extended Activities
Learners find other poems featuring inanimate characters from print or digital sources, identify the characters and explain their significance in the poem.
Conclusion
Learners take an assignment to answer comprehension questions based on the poem, focusing on the inanimate characters used.
ASSESSMENT METHODS
- Reading aloud. - Question and answer. - Learner summaries of what they read. - Peer assessment. - Self-assessment and standardized reading assessments.
LESSON SKILL ASSESSED
i. Identifying inanimate characters in a poem. ii. Finding the meaning of inanimate characters from print and digital texts. iii. Reading a poem and identifying inanimate characters and their role.
WORK DONE
i. Identified inanimate characters in a given poem and supported with text evidence. ii. Found the meaning of inanimate characters from print and digital sources. iii. Read the poem and identified all inanimate characters and what they represent.
REFLECTION

THEME / STRAND / SUB-STRAND
Theme: –
Strand: Listening and Speaking
Sub-strand: Listening Comprehension: Listening for Detail
SPECIFIC LEARNING OUTCOMES
By the end of the lesson, the learner should be able to: a) Select the main idea from a listening passage. b) Respond to questions based on the listening passage. c) Acknowledge the importance of attentive listening.
KEY INQUIRY QUESTION(S)
1. Why should we distinguish between relevant and irrelevant information during a presentation? 2. Why is it important to listen keenly?
LEARNING RESOURCES
~ Course book- Skills in English Learners book and Teachers Guide. ~ Electronic and digital devices. ~ Audio-visual resources. ~ Journals.
ORGANISATION OF LEARNING
Class Organisation: Learners are organised in pairs and later in groups.
Introduction
Introduce listening for detail, explaining the difference between general listening and detailed listening.
Lesson Development
Step 1
Individually, learners pick out details from a listening text such as specific names, dates, events and descriptions.
Step 2
Individually, learners rewrite the passage they listened to in their own words, using the details they noted.
Step 3
In pairs, learners discuss any new information they learnt from the passage, comparing notes with each other.
Step 4
Individually, learners answer questions based on the passage, requiring them to recall specific details.
Extended Activities
Learners use a digital device to search for more information on the topic of the passage and add three more details to their notes.
Conclusion
Learners are guided to summarise the lesson by sharing one detail they found most interesting from the passage.
ASSESSMENT METHODS
- Oral reading or dictation recitations. - Role play. - Debates. - Oral interviews. - Dialogues. - Oral discussions. - Oral presentations. - Public speaking. - Peer assessment. - Self-assessment and standardized listening assessments.
LESSON SKILL ASSESSED
i. Picking out details from a listening text. ii. Rewriting a passage in own words using noted details. iii. Answering questions requiring recall of specific details.
WORK DONE
i. Picked out specific details from a listening text. ii. Rewrote the passage in own words using details noted. iii. Answered questions requiring recall of specific details.
REFLECTION
